topic: Relabeling Standard Physics — vocabulary substitution pattern
our_argument_summary: Section 4.6.2 identifies a pattern across 41 of 64 reviewed WINs: the dome model takes phenomena already predicted and explained by standard physics, renames the causal mechanism (electromagnetic→aetheric, ionospheric→firmament, core dynamics→toroidal flow), and counts the unchanged observation as a 'confirmed prediction.' The section argues this is vocabulary substitution, not prediction, because the dome never predicts a different numerical value than the standard model. It concludes that 'a model that never disagrees with standard physics on any measurable quantity is not an alternative — it is a translation layer.'

holes_found.description: The section claims: 'does the dome model predict a different numerical value than the standard model for any of these [41] phenomena? In every case we reviewed, the answer is no.' This is imprecise to the point of being exploitable. Several WINs tagged relabels_standard=true DO predict different numerical values from their dome geometry — but those predictions are WRONG. WIN-053 (two-pole geomagnetic) predicts B_south ≈ 39 nT from flux conservation, not the observed ~65,000 nT. WIN-067 (Antarctic gravity) predicts 90% gravity variation, not the observed 0.53%. WIN-056 (solar elevation) would predict an arctan curve from dome geometry, not the observed sinusoidal declination. WIN-002/038 (Schumann) would predict ~22 Hz from dome cavity, not 7.83 Hz. In all these cases, the dome abandons its own geometric prediction and adopts the standard model's value instead — which is WORSE than relabeling. The section should distinguish between (a) WINs where the dome never derives a value and just adopts standard physics, and (b) WINs where the dome's geometry predicts a different value that contradicts observation, and the dome silently substitutes the standard value. Category (b) is much more damning and the section currently lumps both together under 'vocabulary substitution.'
holes_found.recommendation: Split the 41 relabels_standard WINs into two sub-categories: 'pure relabeling' (dome never derives its own value, just adopts the standard one with new terminology) and 'silent substitution' (dome geometry predicts a different value, that value is wrong, and the dome reverts to the standard value without acknowledging the failure). The Self-Contradicted WINs with relabels_standard=true (002, 038, 045, 046, 049, 050, 051, 053, 056, 061, 067) are mostly in category (b). This strengthens the argument: the dome doesn't just relabel — it derives wrong values, discards them, and relabels the standard value.

holes_found.description: The 41/64 count may be significantly understated. At least 6 WINs currently tagged relabels_standard=false arguably should be true: WIN-003 (King's Chamber acoustic resonance relabeled as EM dome harmonic), WIN-009 (telluric inter-harmonic minimum relabeled as dome prediction), WIN-011 (Mohe gravimeter reading relabeled as dome EM-gravity coupling), WIN-016 (stellar aberration explained by orbital motion since 1728, relabeled as refractive aether), WIN-029 (ionospheric conductivity relabeled as dome ceiling requirement), WIN-048 (CMB anomaly from standard cosmology relabeled as dome evidence). If these were corrected, the count would be ~47/64 (73%), making the pattern even more dominant. The section's argument is conservative, which is good for credibility but may understate the actual prevalence of relabeling.
holes_found.recommendation: Review the 6 borderline cases (003, 009, 011, 016, 029, 048) and determine if the relabels_standard tag should be corrected. The curmudgeon's Phase 1 reviews for these WINs should document the reasoning. If they are corrected, the section's dynamically-computed count will automatically update — no prose change needed, which is the beauty of computed counts. Even if they're left as-is, the section could note that 41/64 is a conservative lower bound.

holes_found.description: Section 4.6.2 is MISSING from build-doc-v4.js. The HTML version (generate-html.js) contains the full three-paragraph section, but the Word document builder has no corresponding content. This means the downloadable DOCX and PDF are missing this entire argument. Given that some readers may prefer the document format (or may cite the DOCX/PDF as the 'official' version), this is a significant gap in the published review. The CLAUDE.md explicitly warns: 'Updating prose requires editing both files.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add Section 4.6.2 to build-doc-v4.js with equivalent content. Also verify that sections 4.6.1 and 4.6.3 are present in the DOCX builder. If the entire Part 4.6 is missing from the DOCX, that's a larger structural gap.

holes_found.description: The section doesn't preempt the dome defender's strongest rebuttal: 'We're building a unified framework. Individual predictions matching standard physics is expected — the test is whether ONE framework explains ALL 67 phenomena simultaneously. Standard physics needs quantum mechanics, general relativity, geodynamo theory, ionospheric physics, and cosmology — separate theories for separate domains. The dome unifies them under one geometric model.' This is actually a reasonable philosophy-of-science argument (unification IS a scientific virtue, per Kitcher 1981). The section's 'translation layer' metaphor implies that relabeling is inherently empty, but a truly unified model that reproduces all standard predictions from fewer axioms would be scientifically significant — even if individual predictions match. The section should acknowledge this and explain why the dome doesn't meet this bar: it doesn't derive the predictions from a unified set of equations, it adopts them piecemeal.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging the unification argument: 'A genuinely unified model that derives all these predictions from a single geometric framework would be scientifically significant — but the dome model does not do this. It adopts each prediction individually from the standard-physics literature without showing how they follow from dome geometry (only 1 of 64 reviewed WINs derives its value from dome parameters). Relabeling 41 separate standard-physics results as 'aetheric' does not constitute unification.'

holes_found.description: The section makes no reference to specific examples. Unlike SEC-4.6.1 (which discusses the monitoring categories with implicit specificity), SEC-4.6.2 speaks entirely in aggregate. A reader encountering '41 of 64 reviewed WINs' has no way to verify this claim without examining the data themselves. Providing 2-3 concrete examples would make the argument more persuasive and auditable. The Schumann resonance (standard: ionosphere-surface waveguide → dome: 'aetheric cavity'), tidal periods (standard: gravitational mechanics → dome: 'gear-driven luminaries'), and Hubble Law (standard: cosmic expansion → dome: 'aetheric redshift') would be ideal examples spanning different physics domains.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief parenthetical or sentence listing 2-3 specific examples: 'For instance, the Schumann fundamental at 7.83 Hz (derived by Schumann in 1952 from the Earth-ionosphere waveguide) becomes an "aetheric cavity" resonance; tidal periods (known from Doodson's 1921 harmonic analysis) become "gear-driven luminary" oscillations; and the Hubble constant (measured from Type Ia supernovae) becomes an "aetheric redshift" rate — in each case, the predicted value is unchanged.'

holes_found.description: The derives_from_dome tag data strengthens this section's argument but isn't cited. Only 1 of 64 reviewed WINs has derives_from_dome=true. This means 63/64 predictions are NOT derived from dome geometry — they're adopted from elsewhere. Combined with 41/64 relabeling standard physics, the picture becomes: the dome model derives almost nothing from its own geometry, and most of what it claims is standard physics with renamed causes. The section discusses relabeling in isolation when the derives_from_dome statistic would complete the argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Cross-reference the derives_from_dome count: 'This pattern is reinforced by the derivation analysis: only [derives_from_dome count] of [reviewed count] WINs trace their predicted value to dome geometric equations. The remaining predictions are adopted from standard physics literature, renamed, and counted as confirmations.' This creates a one-two punch: most predictions aren't derived from dome geometry (4.6.x) AND most relabel standard physics (4.6.2).

holes_found.description: The phrase 'it is re-narration' at the end of paragraph 2 is rhetorically sharp but imprecise. 'Re-narration' implies telling the same story differently, which could be benign (different theoretical frameworks can narrate the same phenomena). A more precise term would be 're-attribution' (changing the attributed cause without changing the predicted effect) or 'explanatory plagiarism' (claiming credit for a prediction that was already made by another framework). The dome defender could say: 'All models are narrations. General relativity re-narrated Newtonian gravity. Re-narration is how science works.' The section should make clear that the problem isn't re-narration per se, but re-narration without new predictions.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'This is not scientific prediction — it is re-narration' with something like 'This is not scientific prediction — it is re-attribution without new empirical content. When general relativity re-narrated gravity, it also predicted Mercury's perihelion advance and gravitational lensing — phenomena Newtonian gravity could not explain. The dome model's re-narration generates no such novel predictions.'

holes_found.description: The 'translation layer' metaphor in the final sentence is memorable but technically imprecise. A translation layer preserves semantics while changing syntax — it's a neutral operation. The dome model does something worse: it translates the standard model's PREDICTIONS while claiming a fundamentally different ONTOLOGY (flat disc vs. sphere, aether vs. vacuum, local sun vs. distant star). A true translation layer would acknowledge the equivalence. The dome model denies it. A more precise metaphor might be 'rebranding' — presenting an existing product under a new name while denying it's the same product.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider whether 'translation layer' is the right metaphor, or whether 'rebranding' or 'cosmetic relabeling' better captures the deceptive element. The current metaphor might actually be too generous.

stronger_arguments.description: The section should highlight the asymmetry between the dome's treatment of matching vs. non-matching predictions. When dome geometry produces the SAME value as standard physics, it's counted as a WIN. When dome geometry produces a DIFFERENT value (e.g., Schumann ~22 Hz instead of 7.83 Hz, or 90% gravity drop instead of 0.53%), the dome silently abandons its own prediction and adopts the standard value. This selective reporting is more damning than mere relabeling — it reveals that the dome uses standard physics as both the source and the validator of its predictions, making the framework circular and unfalsifiable.

The 'test that would matter' paragraph asks whether the dome predicts different numerical values. But there's an even stronger test: does the dome predict any phenomenon that standard physics CANNOT explain? If the dome merely reproduces standard predictions with new labels, it adds no explanatory power. But if it predicted a genuinely novel phenomenon — an observation that standard physics doesn't predict — that would be scientifically meaningful regardless of the causal vocabulary. The section could note that none of the 67 WINs identify such a phenomenon. Every observation cited by the dome is already in the standard-physics literature.
sources: All 67 WINs — none identify observations unexplained by standard physics

stronger_arguments.description: The relabels_standard pattern interacts with post_hoc in a way the section doesn't exploit. If a WIN is BOTH relabels_standard AND post_hoc (which many are), the dome's 'prediction' consists of: (1) reading a published standard-physics measurement, (2) renaming the cause, and (3) declaring the measurement 'confirmed.' This is a three-step tautology. The section discusses relabeling in isolation, but the combination with post_hoc turns it from 'weak prediction' into 'no prediction at all.'
sources: code_analysis tags: cross-reference relabels_standard=true with post_hoc=true

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's aspiration to unify disparate phenomena under a single geometric framework is not inherently unscientific. Historically, unification has been a productive strategy: Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism, Einstein unified space and time, the Standard Model unifies strong/weak/EM forces. The dome model attempts something similar — deriving geomagnetic, gravitational, optical, and cosmological observations from disc geometry and aetheric parameters. This ambition, while failing in execution, reflects an understanding that good models should explain more with less.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Unification requires deriving predictions from the unified framework's axioms. The dome model doesn't do this — it adopts predictions from the separate standard-physics theories it claims to supersede. With only 1 of 64 reviewed WINs deriving its value from dome geometry, and 41 relabeling standard physics, the unification is nominal, not substantive. Maxwell didn't just rename Coulomb's law and Ampère's law; he derived new predictions (EM waves) from their unification. The dome derives no new predictions from its geometric framework.

citations_verified: Schumann 1952 derivation of 7.83 Hz from Earth-ionosphere waveguide (referenced implicitly — correct)
citations_unchecked: No explicit DOIs or URLs appear in this section — it makes aggregate claims rather than citing specific sources

deeper_analysis:
Section 4.6.2 is the conceptual heart of the code analysis argument: the dome model's predictions are not predictions at all, but relabeled standard physics. The argument is sound and well-structured, but it suffers from three weaknesses that compound each other.

First, it speaks entirely in aggregate without examples, making it harder to verify and less persuasive than it should be. Second, it misses the distinction between 'pure relabeling' (dome never derives its own value) and 'silent substitution' (dome derives a wrong value, discards it, and adopts the standard one). The Self-Contradicted WINs with relabels_standard=true are actually WORSE than relabeling — they demonstrate the dome's own geometry fails, and the dome's response is to import the standard value. Third, it doesn't cross-reference the derives_from_dome tag, which would complete the argument: 63/64 predictions aren't derived from dome geometry, AND 41/64 relabel standard physics. Together, these statistics paint a picture of a model that derives almost nothing from its own framework.

The missing DOCX content is a process issue, not an argument issue, but it means the downloadable document is incomplete — anyone citing the PDF version won't see this section.

The dome defender's strongest counter-argument — unification — is not preempted. The section should acknowledge that a unified framework reproducing standard predictions would be valuable, then demonstrate that the dome doesn't achieve this (piecemeal adoption, not axiomatic derivation).

The 41/64 count appears to be a conservative lower bound. At least 6 WINs currently tagged false have characteristics consistent with relabeling (003, 009, 011, 016, 029, 048). If corrected, the prevalence would rise to ~73%, strengthening the pattern argument. However, being conservative is defensible — better to understate than overstate when attacking a model's credibility.

Overall, the section's core argument is correct and important. The recommended edits (examples, sub-categorization, cross-referencing derives_from_dome, preempting the unification rebuttal, adding DOCX content) would elevate it from a good statistical summary to a devastating structural critique.
